concepts.title: Introduction to Projectile Motion
concepts.conceptBody:
PROJECTICE MOTION

We have already seen that when a particle is given a vertical velocity in earth's gravitational field, it moves along a vertical line. Imagine the motion of a particle when it is given an initial velocity $u$ directed at an angle $\theta $ of with the horizontal and $\theta \ne 90$.

  • Such a particle will move horizontally and as well as vertically i.e. along a curve.
  • For convenience, we will take origin at the point from where the particle is thrown and X-axis, Y-axis as horizontal and vertical respectively.
  • The velocity of particle at any instant is directed along the tangent to the path and can have horizontal and vertical components.
  • The only force acting on the particle is its weight (mg) directed downwards. Hence acceleration is $\text{g}$ directed vertically downwards.
  • As acceleration does not change with time, the projectile motion is a uniformly accelerated motion. At all time instants, ${{\text{a}}_{\text{x}}}=0$ and ${{\text{a}}_{\text{y}}}=-\text{g}$.

Horizontal motion of projectile:

${{\text{a}}_{\text{x}}}=0$

${{\text{v}}_{\text{x}}}={{\text{u}}_{\text{x}}}+0\left( \text{t} \right)$

${{\text{v}}_{\text{x}}}=\text{ucos}\theta $ at all time instants, i.e. the horizontal velocity is constant.

${{\text{s}}_{\text{x}}}=$ horizontal component of displacement in a time interval of $\text{t}$ sec.

${{\text{s}}_{\text{x}}}={{\text{u}}_{\text{x}}}\text{t}+\frac{1}{2}{{\text{a}}_{\text{x}}}{{\text{t}}^{2}}$

$=u\text{cos}\theta \text{(t)}$ (as ${{\text{a}}_{\text{x}}}$ is equal to zero)

horizontal displacement = (horizontal velocity)(time)

Vertical motion of projectile:

${{\text{a}}_{\text{y}}}=-\text{g}$

${{\text{v}}_{\text{y}}}={{\text{u}}_{\text{y}}}-\text{gt}$

${{\text{s}}_{\text{y}}}={{\text{v}}_{\text{y}}}-\frac{1}{2}g{{\text{t}}^{2}}$

$\text{v}_{\text{y}}^{2}=\text{u}_{\text{y}}^{2}-2\text{g}{{\text{s}}_{\text{y}}}$

${{u}_{y}}=$ the vertical component of initial velocity

${{\text{s}}_{\text{y}}}=$ vertical component of displacement during $\text{t}$ sec.

${{\text{v}}_{\text{y}}}=$ vertical component of final velocity

Vertical component of velocity at any time

(i) is zero, if the particle moving horizontally (at the highest point)

(ii) is + ve if it is going up.

(iii) is – ve if it is coming down.

concepts.title: Maximum height
concepts.conceptBody:
Maximum Height 

At point A vertical component of velocity becomes zero, i.e. ${{\text{v}}_{\text{y}}}=0$. Substituting the proper values

$\text{v}_{\text{y}}^{2}=\text{u}_{\text{y}}^{2}+2{{\text{a}}_{\text{y}}}{{\text{s}}_{\text{y}}}$

we have $0={{(u+\sin \alpha )}^{2}}+2(-g)(H)$

$\therefore \text{H}=\frac{{{\text{u}}^{2}}\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\alpha }{2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ g}}$

concepts.title: Range of projectile
concepts.conceptBody:
Horizontal Range (R)

This is equal to the displacement of particle along $x$-axis in time $\text{t}=\text{T}$. Thus, applying ${{\text{s}}_{\text{x}}}={{\text{u}}_{\text{x}}}\text{t}+\frac{1}{2}{{\text{a}}_{\text{x}}}{{\text{t}}^{2}}$, we get

$\text{R}=\left( \text{ucos}\alpha \right)\left( \frac{2\text{usin}\alpha }{\text{g}} \right)+0$

as $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{a}}_{\text{x}}}=0$ and $\text{t}=\text{T}=\frac{2\text{usin}\alpha }{\text{g}}$

$\therefore $ $\text{R}=\frac{2{{\text{u}}^{2}}\text{sin}\alpha \text{cos}\alpha }{\text{g}}=\frac{{{\text{u}}^{2}}\text{sin}2\alpha }{\text{g}}$

or $ $

Here, two points are important regarding the range of a projectile.

(i) Range is maximum where $\text{sin}2\alpha =1$ or $\alpha =45$ and this maximum range is :

${{\text{R}}_{\text{max}}}=\frac{{{\text{u}}^{2}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ g}}$ (at \(\alpha =45\))

(ii) For given value of $u$ range at $\alpha $ and range at $90-\alpha $ are equal although times of flight and maximum heights may be different. Because

concepts.title: Time of Flight of projectile
concepts.conceptBody:
Time of Flight (T) 

Refer Fig. Here, $\text{x}$ and $\text{y}$-axes are in the directions shown in figure. Axis $\text{x}$ is along horizontal direction and axis y is vertically upwards. Thus,

${{\text{u}}_{\text{x}}}=\text{ucos}\alpha ,{{\text{u}}_{\text{y}}}=\text{usin}\alpha ,{{\text{a}}_{\text{x}}}=0$ and ${{\text{a}}_{\text{y}}}=-\text{g}$

At point $B,{{s}_{y}}=0$. So, applying

${{\text{s}}_{\text{y}}}={{\text{u}}_{\text{y}}}\text{t}+\frac{1}{2}{{\text{a}}_{\text{y}}}{{\text{t}}^{2}},\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ we }\!\!~\!\!\text{ have }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

$0=\left( \text{usin}\alpha \right)\text{t}-\frac{1}{2}\text{g}{{\text{t}}^{2}}$

$\text{t}=0,\frac{2\text{usin}\alpha }{\text{g}}$

Both $t=0$ and $t=\frac{2u\text{sin}\alpha }{g}$ correspond to the situation where ${{s}_{y}}=0$. The time $t=0$ corresponds to point $\text{O}$ and time $\text{t}=\frac{2\text{usin}\alpha }{\text{g}}$ corresponds to point $\text{B}$. Thus, time of flight of the projectile is :

$\text{T}={{\text{t}}_{\text{OAB}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

concepts.title: Equation of Trajectory
concepts.conceptBody:
Equation of trajectory

It is the equation of the curve along which the particle moves.

Let the particle move from $\text{O}$ to an arbitary point $\text{P}$ on the curve in time $\text{t}$.

If the coordinates of $\text{P}$ are $\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)$

${{\text{s}}_{\text{x}}}=\text{x}$

${{\text{s}}_{\text{y}}}=\text{y}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}=\left( \text{ucos}\theta \right)\text{t }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\And \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ y}=\left( \text{usin}\theta \right)\text{t}-1/2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ g}{{\text{t}}^{2}}$

eliminating t from two equations;

$y=u\sin \theta \left( \frac{x}{u\cos \theta } \right)-\frac{1}{2}g{{\left( \frac{x}{u\cos \theta } \right)}^{2}}$

$\Rightarrow y=x\tan \theta -\frac{g{{x}^{2}}}{2{{u}^{2}}{{\cos }^{2}}\theta }$ or $y=x\left( 1-\frac{x}{R} \right)\tan \theta$

This is the equation of the curve along which the particle moves. This is called as the equation of the trajectory of the projectile. As $y$ is quadratic polynomial in terms of $x$ i.e. of the form $y=ax-b{{x}^{2}}$, the curve followed by the projectile is a parabola.

concepts.title: Range of Projectile on an inclined plane
concepts.conceptBody:
Range of a Projectile on Inclined Plane

Let OP be an inclined plane making an angle $\beta $ with the horizontal line OX (X-axis). Let a projectile be projected from $\text{O}$ with a speed $\text{u}$ at an angle $\alpha $ with the horizontal. It meets the inclined plane at the point $\text{A}$. The distance $\text{OA}$ is the range on the inclined plane.

Let $\text{R}=\text{OA}$

$\Rightarrow $ A has the coordinates : $\left( \text{Rcos}\beta ,\text{Rsin}\beta \right)$

The equation of trajectory is :

$y=x\text{tan}\alpha -\frac{g{{x}^{2}}}{2{{u}^{2}}\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\alpha }$

$=\frac{x}{u\text{cos}\alpha }-\frac{x}{u\text{cos}\beta }$

$=\frac{x\left( \text{cos}\beta -\text{cos}\alpha \right)}{u\text{cos}\alpha \text{cos}\beta }$

$=\frac{2{{u}^{2}}\left( \text{cos}\beta -\text{cos}\alpha \right)}{t\left( \text{tan}\alpha +\text{tan}\beta \right)u\text{cos}\beta \text{cos}\alpha }$

$=\frac{2u\left( \text{cos}\beta -\text{cos}\alpha \right)}{\text{gsin}\left( \alpha +\beta \right)}$

$=\frac{2u}{g}2\text{sin}\frac{\alpha +\beta }{2}\text{sin}\frac{\alpha -\beta }{2}$

$=2\text{sin}\frac{\alpha +\beta }{2}\text{cos}\frac{\alpha +\beta }{2}$

$=\frac{2u\text{sin}\left( \frac{\alpha -\beta }{2} \right)}{g\text{cos}\left( \frac{\alpha +\beta }{2} \right)}$
